mappings.rationaleAI: Directly addresses the nature-nurture controversy (A.2), covering twin studies, behavioral genetics, gene-environment interaction, and the ethical implications of genetic determinism — mapping precisely to genetic versus environmental factors in human behaviour.

Evolutionary psychology applies evolutionary theory to human cognition and behavior, addressing what capacities are distinctive to humans (A.1.b), phylogenetic continuity (A.1.c), and the nature-nurture debate (A.2) including evolved predispositions versus cultural variation.

Covers neuroplasticity, synaptogenesis, critical periods for language and visual development, and the nature-nurture interplay in infant brain development — directly relevant to environmental versus genetic factors (A.2), cognitive development (A.3.a), and infancy (B.4).

Covers IQ testing, g-factor, multiple intelligence theories, heredity-versus-environment controversy, and comparisons of human and artificial intelligence (A.5), directly addressing cognitive dimensions (A.3.a) and the nature-nurture question (A.2).

Explores the neural basis of memory, perception, and consciousness (A.3.a cognitive dimension), the hard problem of consciousness, free will, and brain research implications — also covers the comparison of human and machine intelligence, relevant to A.5.

Directly addresses the changing concept and experience of childhood, developmental psychology (Piaget), and the stages of human development from Victorian era to present, mapping to early childhood and childhood (B.5) and general aspects of human development (B.1).

Examines whether computers can achieve genuine thought and feeling, the Turing test, computational approaches to consciousness, and the gap between rule-based processing and human understanding — directly addressing comparison of human and artificial intelligence (A.5).

Existentialism's core concerns with freedom, choice, anxiety, and authenticity bear directly on the conative dimension (desires, motivation, the voluntary and involuntary — A.3.b) and the affective dimension (anxiety, the pleasant and unpleasant — A.3.c) of human experience.

Neanderthals exhibited tool-making, burial practices suggesting symbolic thought, and coexistence with Homo sapiens — directly relevant to behavioral capacities shared with other hominins (A.1.a), supposedly distinctive human traits (A.1.b), and phylogenetic continuity (A.1.c).

Covers six million years of human evolution including bipedalism, brain enlargement, tool use, and the Out of Africa hypothesis — directly addressing the evolutionary development of mankind and phylogenetic continuity (A.1.c).

Examines biological mechanisms of ageing including cellular senescence and telomere shortening, as well as the demographic and ethical dimensions of growing older — directly relevant to maturity (B.7) and general aspects of human development (B.1).

Explores the concept of human nature in relation to the natural world, including the nature-culture distinction and the impact of Darwinian evolution on understandings of human nature — relevant to the question of mankind's relationship to nature (A.1).
Human dental evolution and what fossil teeth reveal about evolutionary history directly address the evolutionary development of mankind through anatomical evidence (A.1.c).
